name: FORVM
classification_notes: FORVM Roman draws from later Imperial Roman inscriptions with flared terminals rather than conventional serifs, while FORVM Lineal references early Roman letterforms with near-zero contrast and open endings, placing the pair in classical Roman revival territory.
summary: A two-style family by Étienne Aubert Bonn drawing from two distinct periods of Roman inscribed letterforms, one with near-zero contrast and open terminals, the other with controlled contrast and flared endings.
description: FORVM Lineal looks to the earliest Roman letter cutting, where contrast barely registers and endings splay open, with alternate characters that retain traces of Greek ancestry. FORVM Roman takes a later Imperial model, taming contrast and treating serifs as flared terminals rather than bracketed wedges, giving it classical authority with a quieter finish than Trajan-derived faces. The two styles are designed to work in tandem, their spacing rhythms calibrated to complement one another across centuries of typographic history.
foundry_blurb: Coppers and Brasses describe FORVM as a duo of typefaces inspired by classical Roman inscribed letters. FORVM Lineal references the earliest Roman letterforms, where contrast was almost absent and endings were very open, with alternate characters evoking the curves of letters still close to their Greek ancestors. FORVM Roman is based on a later style, controlling contrast while treating serifs as subtly flared endings rather than the pronounced forms seen on inscriptions like Trajan's Column, giving it a robust, classical feel with a touch of modernity. The two fonts are said to contrast well together, with their spacing variations responding to one another.

name: Racine
classification_notes: Racine has subtle but sharp serifs balanced against organic curves, low contrast, and a very large x-height, all characteristics of a display-oriented serif designed for tight setting at larger sizes.
summary: A display serif by Étienne Aubert Bonn with sharp, spine-like serifs that cut against the softness of its organic curves, creating a characterful texture at tight leading.
description: Designed by Étienne Aubert Bonn at Coppers and Brasses, Racine pairs organic, rounded letterforms with unexpectedly sharp serifs that give the family a spiky, dynamic quality. Characters are weighted towards the top, some appearing almost inverted, which generates an unusual visual rhythm across a line of text. The large x-height and relatively low contrast make it well suited to display use at tight leading, while the generous ink traps at joins add brightness in heavier weights.
foundry_blurb: Coppers and Brasses describe Racine as a typeface with organic forms but sharp thorns — its subtle but cutting serifs balance the overall softness of its curves. Characters tend to sit heavy at the top, some almost appearing upside down, creating a very dynamic texture. Low contrast overall, but generous compensation in the joints adds sparkle, especially in heavier weights. The large x-height makes it well suited as a display face set with tight leading.

name: Mortier
classification_notes: Mortier is a display face derived from hand-painted wall advertising and ghost sign lettering traditions, with squat, rounded-rectangular letterforms that are entirely decorative in function and unsuitable for running text.
summary: A two-style display family drawn from the squat, brick-guided letterforms of hand-painted wall advertisements and ghost signs.
description: Designed by Alexandre Saumier Demers, Mortier takes its cue from pre-digital sign painters who used brick courses as a grid for scale and spacing, producing rounded-rectangular forms that suited the physical constraints of working at height on masonry. The family comes in two extremes: Slim compresses words horizontally while Jumbo stretches them wide, and the two are designed to work in combination. Typical applications are price tags, posters and any setting where vintage commercial lettering is the reference.
foundry_blurb: Coppers and Brasses describe Mortier as inspired by old hand-painted brick-wall advertisements, many of which survive as ghost signs in cities worldwide. Sign painters of the era used the brick and mortar lines as guides for layout and scale, developing squat letterforms with rounded rectangles that were easier to execute on scaffolding than complex curves. The two-style set is built to fill space, with Slim condensing words and Jumbo extending them well beyond a standard text box, and the foundry suggest pairing them together for things like price labels.

name: Agena Display
classification_notes: The foundry describes Agena as bridging 1960s humanist sans-serifs and modern geometric superfamilies, with exaggerated optical corrections and low stroke contrast — placing it between humanist and geometric sans traditions.
summary: A 28-style display superfamily by Étienne Aubert Bonn that spans four widths and seven weights, sitting between humanist and geometric sans traditions.
description: Designed by Étienne Aubert Bonn and released in 2021, the family runs from Condensed to Wide across Light, Book, Regular, Medium, Bold, Extra-Bold and Black. Optical corrections are deliberately exaggerated while stroke contrast is kept low, giving it an unusual tension between geometric rigidity and humanist warmth. The breadth of widths and weights makes it well suited to editorial and poster work where mixing styles within the same family is desirable.
foundry_blurb: Coppers and Brasses describe Agena as a typeface that bridges humanist sans-serifs of the 1960s with contemporary geometric superfamilies. The design exaggerates the optical corrections typical of sans-serifs whilst maintaining relatively low contrast between strokes, achieving a quality that is simultaneously simple, predictable and surprising. The wide range of weights and widths enables varied typographic combinations across 28 styles.

name: Baryton
classification_notes: A display serif with exaggerated, inflated serifs drawn for large-scale use, inspired by the phototypesetting-era Century Bartuska. Its swollen bracketing and unusual proportions place it firmly in the display category rather than text serif.
summary: A two-style display serif drawn in homage to Frank Bartuska's Century Bartuska, with inflated serifs and rounded corners suited to large-scale composition.
description: Designed by Étienne Aubert Bonn and My-Lan Thuong at Coppers and Brasses, Baryton takes its cue from the Photo-Lettering Inc. phototypesetting era, where tight spacing and exaggerated forms became viable at display sizes. The family comprises a roman and a cursive italic, both characterised by swollen serifs, rounded corners and subtle stroke-weight variations that reward close inspection. Intended strictly for large sizes, it carries an elegance unusual for the display register without abandoning its playful, period-aware character.
foundry_blurb: Coppers and Brasses describe Baryton as inspired by the Century Bartuska, a typeface published by Photo-Lettering Inc. in the 1970s that exploited the tight-setting possibilities of phototypesetting. The design expands Bartuska's original concept with the designers' own touch, adding a cursive italic alongside the roman. They note its inflated serifs and unusual widths give the typeface strong personality for a display style, describing it as simultaneously elegant, playful and contemporary, designed exclusively for use at very large sizes.

name: Ilisarniq
classification_notes: A clean, modern sans-serif designed for dual-script use across Latin and Inuktitut syllabics, with humanist proportions optimised for text legibility and tonal consistency between the two scripts.
summary: Designed for the Kativik School Board in Nunavik, this free family spans Latin and Inuktitut syllabics across 10 styles with matched text colour between the two scripts.
description: Commissioned by the Kativik School Board, the only school board in Nunavik, the family was built to serve communities where Inuktitut is the first language of instruction alongside French and English. Étienne Aubert Bonn developed the design with input from the school board and local communities to ensure legibility across both scripts, with particular care taken to match the visual weight of Latin and syllabic text set side by side. The full family of 10 styles runs from Light to Black with italics throughout and is freely available to broaden access to quality Inuktitut syllabic typography.
foundry_blurb: Coppers and Brasses describe Ilisarniq as a modern, clean typeface family created for the Kativik School Board, which operates 17 primary and secondary schools and 5 adult education centres across the 14 communities of Nunavik. The family integrates Latin and Inuktitut syllabic scripts, developed with community involvement to ensure optimal legibility and consistent text colour when both scripts appear together. It is made freely available to improve access to quality Inuktitut syllabic fonts.

name: Triade
classification_notes: A single-weight display typeface with large ink traps and three angle variants (upright, slanted, backslant), designed exclusively for large-scale use — firmly a display face.
summary: A single-weight display typeface in three angle variants — upright, slanted and backslant — with oversized ink traps and a rich set of alternates.
description: Designed by Étienne Aubert Bonn and released in 2016, Triade is built for large-scale use and offers three stylistic angles rather than a range of weights. Enormous ink traps are a deliberate visual feature, and punctuation and diacritics come in two variants — bold and extra-thin — giving the family considerable range within its single weight. Ornamental alternates add further flexibility for display and editorial contexts.
foundry_blurb: Coppers and Brasses describe Triade as an imposing typeface intended strictly for large sizes, with a single weight but three angles: Upright, Slanted and Backslant. The project let them tick off several wishlist items at once — backslant cuts, oversized ink traps and a large set of alternates. Punctuation and diacritics come in both heavy and extra-thin variants, and ornamental alternates are also included.

name: Double
designer: Étienne Aubert Bonn, Alexandre Saumier Demers
classification_notes: A display typeface with a two-axis progression linking weight to width simultaneously, drawn from painted sign lettering traditions. Its extreme proportions and poster orientation place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A five-style display family drawn from painted building advertisements in Old Montreal, where weight and width expand together along a single axis.
description: Inspired by the hand-painted signage covering brick buildings in Vieux-Montréal, the family follows a linked progression: as each style grows heavier, it also grows wider. This means the five styles, from X Small to X Large, each carry a distinct spatial logic suited to different compositional problems. X Small fills space with words; X Large leaves it conspicuously bare.
foundry_blurb: Coppers and Brasses describe Double as an exploration of extremes, citing the painted advertisements of Old Montreal where painters were forced to adapt letter width and weight to fill every corner of a building's façade. The family evolves linearly across two axes simultaneously, so heavier styles are also wider, letting each style dictate its own usage.

name: Canal
classification_notes: Canal is a utilitarian sans-serif with open counters and a warm, humanist character drawn from North American working-class graphic culture of the late 19th and early 20th centuries, placing it firmly in the humanist sans tradition.
summary: A utilitarian sans-serif by Étienne Aubert Bonn, drawn from the working-class graphic vernacular of late 19th and early 20th century North America.
description: Fourteen styles span seven weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, making it a practical choice for both text and light display work. Open counters and a generous width give it a human warmth that keeps it from feeling purely functional. Extended Latin language support and comprehensive OpenType features make it a capable workhorse across a wide range of typesetting tasks.
foundry_blurb: Coppers and Brasses describe Canal as inspired by North American working-class culture of the late 19th and early 20th centuries. Designed by Étienne Aubert Bonn and released in 2015, it is presented as a utilitarian family without embellishment, its width and open counters giving it a particular attitude. The goal was not to reproduce a specific period style but to capture a mood, and the family supports a broad range of Latin languages alongside comprehensive OpenType features.

name: Martha
designer: Étienne Aubert Bonn, Alexandre Saumier Demers
classification_notes: Martha is explicitly described as a fixed-width grotesque, combining the aesthetics of a grotesque sans-serif with monospaced functionality. The curved terminals and lively character give it a grotesque sans identity within a monospaced structure.
summary: A fixed-width grotesque with four weights, curved inward terminals and a complete character set including small capitals.
description: Designed by Étienne Aubert Bonn and Alexandre Saumier Demers and released in 2014, Martha fuses grotesque sans-serif aesthetics with monospaced functionality across Light, Regular, Bold and Black weights. Curved inward terminals give it a lively, distinctive presence that sets it apart from utilitarian coding faces. The unusually complete character set for a monospaced typeface includes small capitals, and even an interrobang.
foundry_blurb: Coppers and Brasses describe Martha as a marriage of grotesque and fixed-width, comparing the combination to peanut butter and jam. Four weights give it the range to handle any fixed-width project, with inward-curving terminals and a bright, vivid presence ensuring text won't go unnoticed. The ridiculously complete character set includes small capitals and an interrobang.
